---
title: "Exercise: Fitting HMMs in R to find CpG islands"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

After this exercise you will be able to:

- Build a 2-state HMM in R with the CRAN
  [`HMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) package (`initHMM`).
- **Simulate** genome-like sequence with `simHMM` and recover its likelihood
  with `forward`.
- **Decode** the most likely hidden-state path with `viterbi` and read it as a
  predicted **CpG-island map**.
- **Learn** the parameters $A$, $B$, $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ from *unlabelled*
  sequence with `baumWelch`, and recognise local optima and label switching.
- Verify one of yesterday's by-hand path probabilities against `simHMM`'s logic.

This is an R-heavy day. Run every block, read the output, and answer the
questions in the comments before opening the solutions.

## Background

We reuse the Day-9 model. States $\{H, L\}$: **H** = "GC-rich
(CpG-island-like)", **L** = "normal background". Symbols are the bases
$\{A, C, G, T\}$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}=\begin{bmatrix}0.5\\0.5\end{bmatrix},\quad
A=\begin{bmatrix}0.8&0.2\\0.3&0.7\end{bmatrix}\ (HL\times HL),\quad
B=\begin{bmatrix}0.1&0.4&0.4&0.1\\0.3&0.2&0.2&0.3\end{bmatrix}\ (ACGT).
$$

The `HMM` package maps one function onto each Day-9 idea: `initHMM` builds the
model, `simHMM` generates data, `forward` gives the likelihood, `viterbi`
decodes the best path, and `baumWelch` learns the parameters. Install it once
with `install.packages("HMM")`.

## Setup

```r
install.packages("HMM")   # run once
library(HMM)

states  <- c("H", "L")
symbols <- c("A", "C", "G", "T")

transProbs <- matrix(c(0.8, 0.2,
                       0.3, 0.7), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                     dimnames = list(states, states))

emissionProbs <- matrix(c(0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1,
                          0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                        dimnames = list(states, symbols))

hmm <- initHMM(States = states, Symbols = symbols,
               startProbs = c(0.5, 0.5),
               transProbs = transProbs,
               emissionProbs = emissionProbs)
hmm   # inspect: check rows of transProbs and emissionProbs each sum to 1
```

## In R

### Task 1 — simulate and inspect

```r
set.seed(1)
sim <- simHMM(hmm, length = 200)

obs   <- sim$observation     # the bases you "see"
truth <- sim$states          # the TRUE hidden path (hidden in real life)

head(obs,   20)
head(truth, 20)

# Q1a: what fraction of the simulated bases are G or C overall?
mean(obs %in% c("G", "C"))

# Q1b: among positions whose TRUE state is H, what fraction are G or C?
#      compare with the L positions. Does it match the emission matrix?
mean(obs[truth == "H"] %in% c("G", "C"))
mean(obs[truth == "L"] %in% c("G", "C"))
```

### Task 2 — likelihood with `forward`

```r
f <- forward(hmm, obs)        # log forward probabilities, states x positions
dim(f)                        # 2 x 200

# total log-likelihood = log-sum-exp of the final column
logsumexp <- function(v) { m <- max(v); m + log(sum(exp(v - m))) }
ll <- logsumexp(f[, length(obs)])
ll
# Q2: this is a LOG probability. Why is the raw probability P(obs) so tiny
#     that we must work in logs? (Recall how fast your Day-9 products shrank.)
```

### Task 3 — decode the islands with `viterbi`

```r
path <- viterbi(hmm, obs)

# Q3a: decoding accuracy against the known truth
mean(path == truth)

# Q3b: read off predicted CpG islands as maximal runs of "H"
runs <- rle(path)
runs

# turn runs into island coordinates (start, end, length) for the H runs
ends   <- cumsum(runs$lengths)
starts <- ends - runs$lengths + 1
islands <- data.frame(state = runs$values, start = starts, end = ends,
                      length = runs$lengths)
islands[islands$state == "H", ]
# Q3c: how many predicted islands are there, and how long is the longest?
```

### Task 4 — learn the parameters with `baumWelch`

Now pretend you never knew $A$ and $B$. Learn them from `obs` alone, starting
from a deliberately uninformative guess.

```r
guess <- initHMM(states, symbols,
                 startProbs    = c(0.5, 0.5),
                 transProbs    = matrix(c(0.6, 0.4,
                                          0.4, 0.6), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                        dimnames = list(states, states)),
                 emissionProbs = matrix(rep(0.25, 8), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                        dimnames = list(states, symbols)))

bw <- baumWelch(guess, obs, maxIterations = 100)

round(bw$hmm$transProbs,    2)   # learned A
round(bw$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)   # learned B
# Q4a: which learned state ended up GC-heavy? Is it called "H" or "L"?
#      (This is LABEL SWITCHING — the name is arbitrary.)
# Q4b: decode with the LEARNED model and compare accuracy to Task 3.
path_learned <- viterbi(bw$hmm, obs)
mean(path_learned == truth)
```

### Task 5 — local optima (a pitfall)

```r
# Try a different starting guess and see if baumWelch lands somewhere else.
guess2 <- initHMM(states, symbols,
                  startProbs    = c(0.5, 0.5),
                  transProbs    = matrix(c(0.9, 0.1,
                                           0.1, 0.9), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                         dimnames = list(states, states)),
                  emissionProbs = matrix(c(0.4, 0.1, 0.1, 0.4,
                                           0.1, 0.4, 0.4, 0.1), 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                         dimnames = list(states, symbols)))
bw2 <- baumWelch(guess2, obs, maxIterations = 100)
round(bw2$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)
# Q5: do bw and bw2 agree (up to label switching)? What does this tell you
#     about trusting a single baumWelch run?
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — building the model"}
[`initHMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) takes `States`, `Symbols`,
`startProbs`, `transProbs`, `emissionProbs`. Use `byrow = TRUE` and `dimnames`
so the matrices read like the page, and double-check **every row sums to 1** —
a row summing to 0.99 is a silent bug. See the package
[reference manual](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/HMM/HMM.pdf).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — simHMM returns two things"}
[`simHMM`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) returns a list:
`$observation` (the emitted symbols, your data) and `$states` (the true hidden
path). You only get `$states` because it is a simulation; on real data the
states are unknown. Use `set.seed()` so your run is reproducible.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — forward gives LOG probabilities"}
[`forward`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) returns a matrix of **log**
forward probabilities (rows = states, columns = positions). The data
log-likelihood is the log-sum-exp of the **last column**. Working in logs avoids
numerical underflow — the same reason your Day-9 products got tiny so fast.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — reading islands off the path"}
[`viterbi`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) returns one state label per
position. [`rle`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/rle.html) (run-length encoding)
collapses consecutive equal labels into runs; the runs whose value is `"H"` are
your predicted CpG islands. `cumsum(runs$lengths)` gives each run's end
coordinate.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 5 — learning and label switching"}
[`baumWelch`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=HMM) returns `$hmm` (the fitted
model) and `$difference` (convergence trace). The fitted state called `"H"` is
**not** guaranteed to be the GC-rich one — decide which is the island state by
reading `bw$hmm$emissionProbs` (the row with high C and G probability), not by
its name. As an alternative package with more flexible emissions, see
[`depmixS4`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=depmixS4).
:::

## Solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}

### Task 1 — simulate and inspect

```r
set.seed(1)
sim <- simHMM(hmm, length = 200)
obs   <- sim$observation
truth <- sim$states

mean(obs %in% c("G", "C"))
# [1] ~0.49        # overall GC content, a mix of both states

mean(obs[truth == "H"] %in% c("G", "C"))
# [1] ~0.80        # H emits C or G with prob 0.4 + 0.4 = 0.8
mean(obs[truth == "L"] %in% c("G", "C"))
# [1] ~0.40        # L emits C or G with prob 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4
```

**Q1a/Q1b:** the per-state GC fractions match the emission matrix ($0.8$ for
$H$, $0.4$ for $L$). The overall fraction sits in between, weighted by how much
time the chain spends in each state.

### Task 2 — likelihood

```r
f  <- forward(hmm, obs)
logsumexp <- function(v) { m <- max(v); m + log(sum(exp(v - m))) }
ll <- logsumexp(f[, length(obs)])
ll
# [1] roughly -270   (a large negative log-probability)
```

**Q2:** $P(\mathbf{x})$ is a product of ~200 emission and ~200 transition
factors, each below 1, so it is around $10^{-117}$ — far smaller than a `double`
can represent. Working with the **log** keeps the number in a sane range; this
is exactly why the by-hand products in Day 9 collapsed toward zero so quickly.

### Task 3 — decoding

```r
path <- viterbi(hmm, obs)
mean(path == truth)
# [1] ~0.88        # most positions decoded correctly; not 100% because
#                  # emissions are genuinely ambiguous

runs <- rle(path)
ends   <- cumsum(runs$lengths)
starts <- ends - runs$lengths + 1
islands <- data.frame(state = runs$values, start = starts, end = ends,
                      length = runs$lengths)
islands[islands$state == "H", ]
#   state start end length
# 1     H     1   7      7
# 5     H    33  41      9
# ...
```

**Q3:** decoding is good but imperfect because a single base never determines
the state; Viterbi uses the surrounding context. The number and length of `H`
runs are your predicted islands (your exact numbers depend on the seed).

### Task 4 — learning

```r
bw <- baumWelch(guess, obs, maxIterations = 100)
round(bw$hmm$transProbs, 2)
round(bw$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)
# one row of emissionProbs becomes GC-heavy (high C and G), the other flatter;
# transProbs drift toward sticky diagonals -- close to the true 0.8/0.7,
# allowing for finite-sample noise and possible label swap.

path_learned <- viterbi(bw$hmm, obs)
mean(path_learned == truth)
# [1] ~0.85        # close to the accuracy from the true model
```

**Q4a:** whichever learned row has high C+G probability **is** the island
state, regardless of whether the package labelled it `"H"` or `"L"` — that is
label switching. **Q4b:** decoding with the learned model recovers the truth
almost as well as the true model, which is the point: we found the structure
from unlabelled sequence.

### Task 5 — local optima

```r
bw2 <- baumWelch(guess2, obs, maxIterations = 100)
round(bw2$hmm$emissionProbs, 2)
```

**Q5:** the two runs usually find essentially the same solution up to label
switching, but not always — EM only reaches a **local** optimum, so a poor start
can land elsewhere. The lesson: run `baumWelch` from **several** initialisations,
compare the resulting log-likelihoods (via `forward`), and keep the best — never
trust a single run blindly.

:::

## Where this leads

You have run the full HMM workflow on genome-like data: build, simulate, score,
decode into an island map, and learn parameters from unlabelled sequence — and
you have seen the pitfalls (local optima, label switching, choosing the number
of states). This **closes the linear-algebra block**.

The ideas continue through the course. In **week 2**, PCA replaces discrete
hidden states with continuous latent directions found via the eigenvectors of
days 5–6. The **Bayesian-statistics** block generalises today's "infer the
hidden from the observed" with priors and full posteriors. And **single-cell**
analysis will segment and annotate cells much as you segmented and annotated a
genome here. You now hold the complete linear-algebra toolkit the rest of the
course rests on.
